titlestmt.titleproper: Guide to the Michael D. Heaston Collection of Western Land and Settlement Manuscripts
Heaston (Michael D.) Collection of Western Land and Settlement Manuscripts
titlestmt.author: Sarah Lerner

publicationstmt.publisher: Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library

maintenanceagency.agencyname: Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library

maintenanceevent.eventdescription: This finding aid was produced using ArchivesSpace on Wednesday August 19, 2020 at 05:35

did.unittitle: Michael D. Heaston Collection of Western Land and Settlement Manuscripts

corpname.part: Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library

did.abstract:
Single letters, small groups of letters, and other manuscripts created by various individuals and assembled by collector Michael D. Heaston pertaining to western American land and settlement, 1846-1939 and undated. Letters discuss real property, economic conditions, and frontier and pioneer life in Alabama, Colorado, Iowa, Kansas, Oklahoma, Minnesota, Missouri, Montana, Nebraska, South Dakota, and Texas. Some letters include pictorial letterheads with maps.
Topics covered in the collection include agriculture, Native Americans, oil, missionary activity, ranching, and slavery.

accessrestrict.head: Conditions Governing Access
accessrestrict.p: This collection is open for research.

acqinfo.head: Immediate Source of Acquisition
acqinfo.p: Purchased from Michael D. Heaston on the Frederick W. and Carrie S. Beinecke Fund for Western Americana, 2006.

prefercite.head: Preferred Citation
prefercite.p: Michael D. Heaston Collection of Western Land and Settlement Manuscripts. Yale Collection of Western Americana, Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library, Yale University.

processinfo.head: Processing Information
processinfo.p: Collections are processed to a variety of levels, depending on the work necessary to make them usable, their perceived research value, the availability of staff, competing priorities, and whether or not further accruals are expected. The library attempts to provide a basic level of preservation and access for all collections, and does more extensive processing of higher priority collections as time and resources permit. These materials have been arranged and described according to national and local standards. For more information, please refer to the Beinecke Manuscript Unit Processing Manual.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Single letters, small groups of letters, and other manuscripts created by various individuals and assembled by collector Michael D. Heaston pertaining to western American land and settlement, 1846-1939 and undated. Letters discuss real property, economic conditions, and frontier and pioneer life in Alabama, Colorado, Iowa, Kansas, Oklahoma, Minnesota, Missouri, Montana, Nebraska, South Dakota, and Texas. Some letters include pictorial letterheads with maps.
Topics covered in the collection include agriculture, Native Americans, oil, missionary activity, ranching, and slavery.

userestrict.head: Conditions Governing Use
userestrict.p: The Michael D. Heaston Collection of Western Land and Settlement Manuscripts is the physical property of the Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library, Yale University. Literary rights, including copyright, belong to the authors or their legal heirs and assigns. For further information, consult the appropriate curator.

geogname.part: San Luis Valley (Colo. and N.M.) -- Maps

subject.part: Real property -- South Dakota -- Clear Lake

corpname.part: Presbyterian Church in the U.S.A.. Board of Home Missions
Atchison, Topeka, and Santa Fe Railway Company
Missouri, Kansas and Texas Railway Company

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Autograph letter, signed, from Lexington, Missouri, to Whitney Clark regarding William Hay's return to Lexington. William Hay writes of family illness and recovery, as well as his sucess in the fanning mill business. He notes that volunteers are leaving for Santa Fe, New Mexico, with farmers selling their belongings to the government "at fine prices". He also notes his distaste for United States President James K. Polk. Following William Hay's letter, Martha Hay writes to her sister Minerva Clark discussing family matters.

odd.head: General
odd.p: Accompanied by a typed transcript made by an unidentified transcriber.

did.unittitle: Parks, John and G. W. Ropston

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Autograph letter, signed, from Washington, Arkansas, to Ephram Lynch concerning Parks's time in Texas. Following Parks's letter is another from G. W. Ropston in Clarksville, Texas, regarding prices assigned to enslaved African and African-American men and women in Texas.

odd.head: General
odd.p: Accompanied by a typed transcript made by an unidentified transcriber.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Autograph letter, signed, from Mobile, Alabama, to Henry S. Randall in which Evans purchases sheep from Randall. Evans writes of the conditions for his sheep in Mobile.

did.unittitle: Holt, George E. and Lora H. Holt

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Autograph letter, signed, from Lawrence City, Kansas, to Ellery C. Pike in which Lora H. Holt outlines her journey from Willamantic, Connecticut, to Lawrence City. Holt writes of the condition of the city, noting that there exists only one log building and the citizens live in tents. She adds that George E. Holt has built for them a log cabin and proceeds to describe it; she also notes that Native Americans come to the city to trade. Following Lora Holt's letter, George E. Holt writes that Kansas will likely be settled soon and hopes that it becomes a free state.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Autograph letter, signed, from Omaha City, Nebraska, to "Friend Clark" regarding pre-emption claims and the value of Jones's new land. Jones writes of the United States Congress's appropriation of funds for a wagon road to the Pacific Coast from Fort Kearney, as well as a penitentiary in Omaha City.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Autograph letter, signed, from Clinton, Iowa, to "Dear Father and Mother" regarding Woodruff's property in Clinton. Woodruff writes of the development and value of town lots.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Autograph letter, signed, from Topeka, Kansas, to sister Betsey McGuire concerning the Palmer family's living conditions in Topeka. Palmer writes of his construction of a home and having planted vegetables. He provides prices for corn and potatoes, flour, butter, eggs, and green apples; he also notes wages for common laborers and stone masons.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Autograph letter, signed, from Brownville, Nebraska, to Abijah Castine encouraging him to move to Brownville. Hoadly writes of the town's progress.

odd.head: General
odd.p: Accompanied by a typed transcript made by an unidentified transcriber.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Autograph letter, signed, from Burr Oak, Iowa, to "Dear Friends R & C" concerning "Lydia's" travels from Cleveland, Ohio, to Burr Oak.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Autograph letter, signed, from Fort Kearny, Nebraska, to "Dear people at house" regarding an upcoming homeward march to Fort Leavenworth, Kansas. "Bud" includes prices for potatoes, onions, and butter in Nebraska.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Autograph letter, signed, from Celina, Minnesota, to "Brother Perry" regarding Miller's living situation in Celina.

did.unittitle: Atchison, Topeka, and Santa Fe Railroad Company

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: 2 printed reports, completed in manuscript and signed, regarding lands sold by the Atchison, Topeka, and Santa Fe Railroad Company's Land Department. Recorded are the number of acres sold, amount of sales, and amount of cash received.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Autograph letter, signed, from Algona, Kossuth County, Iowa, to "My dear Hanks" concerning the recipient's interest in purchasing western land for a homestead. Hudnutt advises the recipient not to purchase property in Iowa as it is too cold and instead suggests the Mississippi Valley, Oregon, California, or Missouri. He writes that much of the land suited for agriculture has been claimed and that "the day is not far distant when the rich men will monopolize the good land".

odd.head: General
odd.p: Accompanied by a typed transcript made by an unidentified transcriber.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Autograph letter, signed, from Austin, Texas, to Hardin B. Adams concerning the readmittance of Texas to the Union. Clay also writes of his living situation in Austin.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: 19 autograph letters, signed, from real estate companies including A. C. Pierce of Junction City, Kansas; Webb & Hill of Albany, Texas; A. F. Rowland of Sioux Falls, Dakota; Lycurgus Smith of Mineral Wells, Texas; D. Richardson; McClary & Fisher of Lacon, Illinois; William I. Noble of Clear Lake, South Dakota; I. N. Pepper's Agency of Stockton, Kansas; G. F. McCloskey of Galena, South Dakota; St. Joseph Real Estate Exchange Co. of Saint Joseph, Missouri; C. W. Miller of Hays County, Kansas; N. P. Dodge & Co. of Council Bluffs, Iowa; Brunner & Baird of Mankato, Minnesota; Rothrock & Wolf of Lipton, Iowa; and E. L. Stone of Sioux County, Iowa. Letters chiefly concern property sales. Pictorial letterheads include maps of Junction City, Albany, Sioux Falls, and Clear Lake.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Autograph letter, signed, from Limestone County, Texas, to Lucien L. Reese regarding Reese's plans to move to Texas. McKissock writes that the best land in Texas is east of the Colorado River and north of the San Antonio Railroad; he proposes Grayson County, Cook County, Montague County, or Denton County. He offers Reese the opportunity to purchase interest in his store in Kosse with business partner A. F. McKissock. He also comments on carpetbaggers and scalawags.

did.unittitle: Missouri, Kansas and Texas Railway Company

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Manuscript copy of a letter from lawyers Britton & Gray to George Denison, vice president of the Missouri, Kansas and Texas Railway Company, concerning the "Boyd" decision, which related to pre-emption claims, homestead claims, and railroad grants.

Typescript petition, signed, to A. H. Garland, Attorney General of the United States, concerning owners of the land in controversy in the suit of the United States vs. the Missouri, Kansas and Texas Railway Company.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Autograph letter, signed, from Halletsville, Texas, to E. D. Hicks regarding the status of A. W. Hicks's family. Hick also notes an opportunity to purchase a 4,800 acre property with portions suited to ranching.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Autograph letter, signed, from Fort Assinniboine, Montana, to "My darling Darling Julie" concerning "Galtin's" admiration for the recipient. The author also writes of Fort Assinniboine and of Native Americans in its vicinity.

odd.head: General
odd.p: Accompanied by a typed transcript made by an unidentified transcriber.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Autograph letter, signed, from Pueblo, Colorado, to "Cousin Ross" in which Dana writes of his plans to head west in search of work. He notes that he is used to seeing guns and knives in the area, having spoken to a leader of the "mob men". Dana also notes that the people in Leadville are starving and without provisions.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: 10 autograph letters, signed, from Waxahachie, Texas, chiefly to Calvin Thraxton concerning real estate in Mason County, Texas. Neal was a surveyor and general land agent.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Autograph letter, signed, from Matagorda County, Texas, to "Messrs. Lawrence and Edwards" regarding Braman's property in Concho County. Braman writes that he cannot afford to sell his lands for less than one dollar per acre.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Autograph letter, signed, from "Winsors Ranch, I.T." to Leonard Swett and Laura Quigg Swett regarding Leonard Herbert Swett's efforts rounding up cattle and transporting cutting horses.

odd.head: General
odd.p: Accompanied by a typed transcript made by an unidentified transcriber.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Typescript remarks, signed, concerning an order announced by W. A. J. Sparks, Commissioner of the General Land Office. Twitchell writes that the official act--the suspension of final action on land entries--has negatively affected pioneers in Kansas, Nebraska, Colorado, "Dakota", Idaho, Utah, New Mexico, Washington, Montana, Wyoming, Nevada, and Minnesota.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Autograph letter, signed, from Washington, D. C., to Abner Taylor regarding the "Cherokee strip" becoming open to land settlement. Logan writes that he will speak to Senator Preston B. Plumb concerning the matter.

odd.head: General
odd.p: Accompanied by a partial typed transcript made by an unidentified transcriber.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Autograph letter, signed, from Weeping Water, Nebraska, to James Sand ("Friend Jimbo") in which Jessop tells the recipient about Nebraska. Jessop writes of having taking a job with the railroad, of considering the purchase of a timber claim and homestead, and of economic prospects. A second autograph letter, signed, from Manley, Nebraska, to an unidentified recipient concerns a cyclone in Iowa and corn in Nebraska. Accompanied by a leather billfold.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Autograph letter, signed, from Washington, D.C., to J. B. Harrison (an agent of the Indian Rights Association) concerning Harrison's suggestion of postponing the "Oklahoma bill". Springer writes that the condition of Oklahoma, or "no man's land", is such that immediate legislation is required--he notes that 10,000 to 15,000 individuals have settled in this territory and are without titles, government, or law. Springer adds that no Native Americans occupy the land and encourages Harrison to pass the bill in an upcoming session. Accompanied by 2 newspaper clippings regarding Springer's health, undated.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Typescript letter, signed, from Estacado, Crosby County, Texas, to the Munson brothers concerning their property in Swisher County, Texas. Earnest & Shephard write that they "are making an extra exertion [...] to induce immigration to this section" and hope to purchase the Munsons' land.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Autograph contract, signed, between E. H. Gillette and William E. Nichols securing payment for investment in franchises located in Oklahoma City, Indian Territory. Contract created in Des Moines, Iowa.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Autograph letter, signed, from Watonga, Oklahoma, to brother Dewey Holden regarding Vincent's homestead in Oklahoma. Vincent writes that the Board of Home Missions commissioned him as a missionary but offered him less money than promised. He adds that to keep his land claim, he must build a house and send for his family, but lacks the funds to do so. Vincent also writes of having received donations from acquaintances and of his intent to secure a field in a nearby state.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Autograph letter, signed, from San Antonio, Texas, to "My dear Scott" regarding the author's decision to go into partnership with a rancher in Uvalde, Texas. The author notes his difficulty in securing a sleeper car west for a trip to Los Angeles, California, due to the volume of travel in that direction. On pictorial letterhead for the Menger, a health resort.

did.unittitle: Williams, J. T., J. M. Partridge, and C. Williams

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Typescript articles of co-partnership, signed, for the formation of Williams, Partridge & Company, an organization seeking to locate settlers on the Crow Reservation.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Typescript letter, signed, from Del Norte, Colorado, to S. M. Wilson advertising land for sale in Colorado. Beidler writes of the health benefits of the climate. He also notes the ability to grow potatoes and small grains. Accompanied by a printed map, produced by Beidler, of San Luis Valley.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: 6 typescipt letters, signed, from the Magnolia Land Company of Houston, Texas, to J. H. Kramer. Owen A. Wood, company founder, writes of the profitability of Texas oil. A printed map shows oil wells in the Fresno area; a broadside advertises oil opportunities in Houston.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Typescript document, signed, in which the undersigned agree to pay a set amount for the purpose of securing an extension of the Wichita Falls and Northwestern Railway from Altus, Oklahoma, to Wellington, Texas.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Manuscript document, unsigned, concerning 600,000 acres of land in Hidalgo County, Texas. Noted is the profitability of sugar, cotton, and other crop production in the area. Included is a review of accessibility--a potential railroad line from Matamoros, Mexico, to Mazatlán, Mexico, is referenced as is a donation of 10,240 acres from Texas for the purpose of constructing a railroad beginning in Houston.
